description: CurlBro exercise catalog — strength, stretching, and mobility exercises for gym workout building

exercises.name: Bodyweight Squat (Air Squat)
exercises.notes: The starting point for all beginners. Great warmup before heavy squats.

exercises.name: Goblet Squat
exercises.notes: The front-loaded weight prevents leaning forward. Perfect learning tool. Practical loading ceiling — most people outgrow it and move to barbell or leg press.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Squat
exercises.notes: Simpler grip than goblet squat for heavier loads.

exercises.name: Barbell Back Squat
exercises.notes: The king of leg exercises. Wider stance increases adductor demand, narrower increases knee travel and quad ROM. If you can only do one leg exercise, do this.

exercises.name: Barbell Front Squat
exercises.notes: More quad-focused and easier on the lower back than back squat. The upright torso makes it self-correcting. Difficulty is intermediate, not advanced — the cross-arm grip makes it accessible.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Squat
exercises.notes: Safe solo squatting option. The fixed bar path removes the balance demand, letting you focus on pushing hard.

exercises.name: Hack Squat Machine
exercises.notes: Foot placement changes everything: high/wide = glute emphasis, low/narrow = quad emphasis.

exercises.name: Pendulum Squat (Lever Squat)
exercises.notes: Increasingly common in well-equipped gyms. The arc path loads quads through a deeper stretch than a hack squat, with zero spinal compression. One of the best pure quad machines available.

exercises.name: Belt Squat
exercises.notes: Zero spinal compression — the best squat option for anyone with back issues or for adding squat volume without taxing recovery. Allows heavy loading with the spine completely unloaded. Not every gym has one, but more are adding them.

exercises.name: Leg Press (45-Degree)
exercises.notes: Handles very heavy weight with almost zero spinal stress. High foot = more glute, low foot = more quad.

exercises.name: Seated Leg Press (Horizontal)
exercises.notes: Many gyms have these alongside 45-degree presses. The horizontal path creates a slightly different resistance curve — tends to be harder at the bottom. Very beginner-friendly since you sit upright.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Leg Press
exercises.notes: Best way to find and fix left/right strength imbalances with measurable loading. Much more stable than single-leg squats — the machine handles the balance for you.

exercises.name: Leg Extension Machine
exercises.notes: The only machine that fully isolates quads. Great finisher after squats or leg press. Note: the seated position shortens the rectus femoris at the hip, so it primarily loads the vasti muscles. Pair with reverse nordics or sissy squats for complete quad coverage.

exercises.name: Reverse Nordic Curl
exercises.notes: The quad counterpart to the Nordic hamstring curl. Loads the rectus femoris at long muscle lengths, which leg extensions cannot do. Growing research support for both quad hypertrophy and patellar tendon health (Alonso-Fernandez et al., 2023). Superset with Nordic curls for a brutal bodyweight leg finisher.

exercises.name: Sissy Squat
exercises.notes: Old-school quad exercise. No equipment needed. Similar to reverse Nordic but on toes instead of kneeling. Be cautious if you have existing knee pain — but for healthy knees, loading in deep knee flexion is generally safe and beneficial.

exercises.name: Spanish Squat (Banded)
exercises.notes: Widely used in physio and sport science for patellar tendon rehab and quad strengthening. The band deloads the knee joint while keeping high quad demand. Great for warmups, prehab, or adding quad volume with minimal joint stress.

exercises.name: Walking Lunge (Dumbbell)
exercises.notes: Longer stride = more glute, shorter stride = more quad.

exercises.name: Reverse Lunge (Dumbbell)
exercises.notes: Recommended over forward lunges for beginners — less knee stress and easier to balance.

exercises.name: Stationary Lunge (Split Squat)
exercises.notes: Simplest lunge variation. Perfect starting point before walking or Bulgarian.

exercises.name: Bulgarian Split Squat
exercises.notes: Research shows these build quads as well as back squats (Speirs et al., 2016). Excellent for fixing left/right imbalances. Also loads glutes in a deep stretch which may be superior for hypertrophy.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Step-Up
exercises.notes: Higher box = more glute. Very functional — mimics stair climbing. Common mistake: pushing off with the trailing leg, which defeats the purpose.

exercises.name: Barbell Hip Thrust
exercises.notes: Research shows very high peak glute activation (Contreras et al.). However, exercises that load glutes in a deep stretch — like deep squats, Bulgarian split squats, and RDLs — may produce comparable or superior glute growth. Best used alongside stretch-focused exercises, not instead of them.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Hip Thrust
exercises.notes: Easier to get into position than free barbell. The Smith bar stays in place — no rolling. Great for beginners who find barbell hip thrust setup awkward.

exercises.name: Glute Bridge
exercises.notes: Perfect warmup for activating glutes before squats/deadlifts. Add a barbell across hips for more resistance. Same movement pattern as hip thrust, just from the floor.

exercises.name: Glute Drive Machine (Hip Thrust Machine)
exercises.notes: The glute drive machine (e.g., Nautilus Glute Drive) is a plate-loaded hip thrust machine that eliminates the setup complexity of barbell hip thrusts — no bench positioning, no barbell pad, no rolling the bar into place. Research by Contreras et al. (2015, 2020) consistently shows hip thrusts produce among the highest glute EMG activation of any exercise, rivaling or exceeding squats and deadlifts for the gluteus maximus. The machine variant provides a fixed back pad and hip belt, making it easier to load progressively and reducing lower back involvement compared to barbell hip thrusts. The movement is primarily concentric/eccentric hip extension with minimal spinal loading, making it joint-friendly for lifters with back issues. Ideal placement: after heavy compounds (squats, deadlifts) for targeted glute work, or as a primary movement on a glute-focused day.

exercises.name: Cable Pull-Through
exercises.notes: If you can't feel your glutes/hamstrings during deadlifts, do these first to learn the pattern. Can also serve as a primary hip hinge for beginners not ready for barbell work.

exercises.name: Cable Glute Kickback
exercises.notes: Targets one glute at a time. Constant cable tension throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Sumo Squat (Dumbbell)
exercises.notes: Wide stance hits inner thighs (adductors) and glutes more than regular squat.

exercises.name: Hip Adduction Machine
exercises.notes: Often overlooked but important for inner thigh strength and knee stability.

exercises.name: Hip Abduction Machine
exercises.notes: Targets glute medius — important for hip stability and preventing knee cave during squats.

exercises.name: Banded Clamshell
exercises.notes: Excellent glute medius activation for warmups and prehab. Do 2 sets per side before squatting to wake up the glute med and reduce knee cave. Can be done anywhere with a cheap band.

exercises.name: Pistol Squat (Single-Leg Squat)
exercises.notes: The gold standard of single-leg bodyweight strength. Requires full ankle dorsiflexion, hip flexor flexibility (to hold the extended leg), and exceptional quad and glute strength. Progress from single-leg box squat → assisted pistol → full pistol. Not inherently dangerous for knees — research supports deep single-leg squatting as safe for healthy joints.

exercises.name: Shrimp Squat
exercises.notes: Demands more quad strength at deep flexion than a pistol squat because the rear leg is held behind rather than in front. Less hip flexor demand than pistols. Great progression after mastering single-leg box squats. Emphasizes knee flexion ROM and quad control at long muscle lengths.

exercises.name: Wall Sit
exercises.notes: Simple isometric quad exercise requiring zero equipment. Isometric training at long muscle lengths has been shown to produce hypertrophy (Oranchuk et al., 2019). Also used in patellar tendon rehab protocols. Great finisher or warmup. Progress by adding time, then by holding a dumbbell or plate on your lap.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Box Squat
exercises.notes: The best regression for the pistol squat. The box provides a safety net and allows partial ROM at first. Progress by lowering the box height over weeks. Excellent for building single-leg strength and balance in a controlled way. No equipment beyond a sturdy box or bench needed.

exercises.name: Double Kettlebell Front Squat
exercises.notes: Excellent intermediate option bridging goblet squats and barbell front squats. The rack position builds thoracic extension and core bracing. Popular in kettlebell sport and functional fitness. Two moderate KBs often provide more load than a single heavy KB in goblet position.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Goblet Lunge
exercises.notes: The goblet hold naturally limits forward lean and teaches proper upright lunge mechanics. Good alternative when dumbbells at sides cause grip fatigue before legs fatigue. Pairs well with other kettlebell work in circuit-style training.

exercises.name: Banded Squat
exercises.notes: Great for home workouts, warmups, or travel. The band provides accommodating resistance (harder at top, easier at bottom). Adding a loop band above the knees during squats increases glute medius activation and helps cue proper knee tracking. Practical ceiling is low — progress to weighted squats when possible.

exercises.name: Banded Lateral Walk
exercises.notes: Excellent warmup and glute medius activation drill. More functional than seated abduction machine because it trains the glute med in a standing, dynamic position. Common in physical therapy and sport performance. Place band above knees for more glute medius, around ankles for more TFL and overall difficulty.

exercises.name: V-Squat Machine
exercises.notes: The V-squat (or reverse hack squat) positions you face-first against the pad, creating a pendulum-like arc that differs from standard hack squats. Provides excellent quad stretch at the bottom. Zero spinal compression. Growing popularity in bodybuilding-focused gyms. Uses the hack squat machine type since most V-squat machines share the same basic design.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Lunge
exercises.notes: The Smith machine removes the balance component of lunges, making them more accessible for beginners and allowing heavier loading. Excellent for those who struggle with dumbbell lunge balance. The fixed path keeps you locked in, so focus on depth and knee tracking.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Split Squat
exercises.notes: Combines the deep stretch and single-leg emphasis of a Bulgarian split squat with the stability of the Smith machine. Allows heavier progressive overload than dumbbell BSS since grip is not the limiting factor. Popular in bodybuilding for targeted quad and glute hypertrophy.

exercises.name: Landmine Squat
exercises.notes: The landmine creates a natural arc that accommodates different body proportions and encourages deep, upright squatting. Lower spinal stress than barbell back squats because the load is anterior and the bar path moves slightly away from vertical. Good bridge between goblet squats and barbell squats. Only requires a barbell and a corner — no rack needed.

exercises.name: Zercher Squat
exercises.notes: An old-school lift named after Ed Zercher. Forces the most upright torso of any barbell squat variation, with very high core and upper back demand. The low bar position shifts load away from the spine. Excellent for lifters with shoulder mobility issues preventing front rack or back rack positions. The elbow discomfort is the main barrier — it diminishes with practice and padding.

exercises.name: Jefferson Squat (Straddle Deadlift)
exercises.notes: A hybrid squat-deadlift pattern with built-in anti-rotation. Named after 19th-century strongman Charles Jefferson. The straddle stance creates asymmetric loading that challenges the core in rotation and lateral flexion — something bilateral squats don't do. Growing interest in strength sports for its carryover to athleticism and injury resilience. Less spinal shear than conventional deadlifts due to the more vertical torso.

exercises.name: Lateral Lunge (Side Lunge)
exercises.notes: One of the few exercises that trains legs in the frontal (side-to-side) plane. Most leg training is sagittal (forward/back). Lateral lunges stretch the adductors of the straight leg while strengthening the quads and glutes of the stepping leg. Important for athletes in sports requiring lateral movement (tennis, basketball, soccer). Also excellent for hip mobility.

exercises.name: Barbell Forward Lunge
exercises.notes: Heavier loading than dumbbell lunges because the barbell allows more total weight. The forward step requires more deceleration force on the front leg, increasing quad demand compared to reverse lunges. Requires good balance and core stability. Perform in a squat rack for safety. Shorter steps emphasize quads, longer steps shift load to glutes and hamstrings.

exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Lunge
exercises.notes: The barbell-loaded version of the reverse lunge allows heavier progressive overload than the dumbbell variant. Stepping backward is more knee-friendly because the shin stays more vertical on the working leg, reducing anterior knee shear force. Smaller step = more quad emphasis, larger step with heel drive = more glute/hamstring emphasis. Preferred over forward lunges for athletes with knee issues.

exercises.name: Conventional Deadlift
exercises.notes: Works more muscles than any other exercise. Very taxing — don't do heavy deadlifts and heavy squats same day when starting out.

exercises.name: Sumo Deadlift
exercises.notes: Shorter range of motion, easier on lower back. Great for people with longer torsos. Higher adductor demand than conventional.

exercises.name: Trap Bar Deadlift (Hex Bar)
exercises.notes: THE BEST deadlift for beginners. Neutral grip, centered weight, lower injury risk. Start here if your gym has one.

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift (Barbell RDL)
exercises.notes: Essential hamstring/glute builder. Different from conventional deadlift — you start standing, not from the floor. Loads hamstrings in a deep stretch, which is a potent hypertrophy stimulus.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Best entry point for learning the hip hinge before graduating to barbell.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Amazing for fixing left/right imbalances and building hip stability. Pair with single-leg leg press for a complete unilateral leg session.

exercises.name: Stiff-Leg Deadlift
exercises.notes: Greater hamstring stretch than RDL. More lower back demand — keep weight lighter than your RDL.

exercises.name: Good Morning
exercises.notes: Excellent posterior chain builder. Demands good hip hinge mechanics. Commonly used as a squat accessory in powerlifting programs.

exercises.name: Rack Pull
exercises.notes: Overloads the top half of the deadlift. Great for back/trap development and grip training.

exercises.name: Lying Leg Curl Machine
exercises.notes: Simple hamstring isolation. Pair with RDLs for complete hamstrings — curls hit the knee-flexion function, RDLs hit the hip-extension function.

exercises.name: Seated Leg Curl Machine
exercises.notes: Research suggests seated may be slightly better for hamstring growth than lying — the seated position places hamstrings in a more stretched starting position at the hip (Maeo et al., 2021). If your gym has both, prefer seated.

exercises.name: Nordic Hamstring Curl
exercises.notes: Gold standard for hamstring injury prevention — strong research backing (van der Horst et al., 2015). Start with just the slow lowering (eccentric) phase. Superset with reverse Nordic curls for a brutal bodyweight quad/hamstring finisher.

exercises.name: Stability Ball Leg Curl
exercises.notes: No machine needed. Adds core stability challenge. Keep hips high throughout — if they drop, the exercise gets easier but less effective.

exercises.name: Back Extension (Roman Chair / Hyperextension)
exercises.notes: Important for lower back endurance and health. Hold a plate for more resistance. Can bias toward glutes by rounding upper back slightly and squeezing glutes at top, or bias toward erectors by keeping a flat back throughout. Trains hip extension, spinal extension, and erector endurance simultaneously.

exercises.name: Standing Calf Raise Machine
exercises.notes: Straight knees target the gastrocnemius (visible calf muscle). Full range of motion is key — don't bounce.

exercises.name: Seated Calf Raise Machine
exercises.notes: Bent knee targets soleus (deeper calf muscle under the gastrocnemius). Do BOTH standing AND seated for complete calves. The soleus is actually a larger muscle than the gastrocnemius.

exercises.name: Calf Press on Leg Press
exercises.notes: Efficient — do right after leg press sets without moving. Straight legs = gastrocnemius focus.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Can be done anywhere with a step. Great for fixing calf imbalances between legs.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Swing
exercises.notes: Excellent for conditioning and explosive hip power. Form matters more than weight. Master the cable pull-through or RDL hip hinge pattern first.

exercises.name: Glute-Ham Raise (GHR)
exercises.notes: One of the best hamstring exercises — trains both knee flexion and hip extension simultaneously, mimicking how hamstrings function in sprinting. The eccentric loading is excellent for injury prevention (Schoenfeld, 2010). Requires a dedicated GHR machine or a modified roman chair.

exercises.name: Reverse Hyperextension
exercises.notes: Made popular by Louie Simmons at Westside Barbell. Decompresses the spine under light load while strengthening glutes and hamstrings — excellent for lifters with back issues. Unlike back extensions, the torso is fixed and the legs move, which reduces spinal loading.

exercises.name: Standing Hip Extension Machine
exercises.notes: Simple glute isolation that is easy on the lower back. Great for beginners learning to activate glutes before progressing to hip thrusts. The standing position allows a good glute stretch at the bottom of the movement.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Kettlebell Swing
exercises.notes: Adds anti-rotation core demand and grip challenge compared to the two-arm version. The asymmetric loading trains obliques and improves rotational stability. Master the two-arm swing first before progressing to single-arm.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: The centered load position (between legs) allows a deeper hip hinge than dumbbells at the sides. Great for home gyms where a barbell is not available. Pairs naturally with goblet squats for a minimalist kettlebell leg session.

exercises.name: Sliding Leg Curl (Towel / Sliders)
exercises.notes: Excellent at-home hamstring exercise requiring no equipment. The eccentric phase is very demanding — similar stimulus to Nordic curls but more accessible. Keep hips elevated throughout for maximum hamstring engagement. Progression: two-leg slide out, single-leg curl back.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
exercises.notes: Unilateral glute exercise requiring zero equipment. Excellent for identifying and correcting left-right glute imbalances. Progresses naturally to single-leg hip thrusts. Can be used as a glute activation drill before heavy squats or deadlifts.

exercises.name: Donkey Calf Raise
exercises.notes: The bent-hip position pre-stretches the gastrocnemius more than a standing calf raise, potentially offering a superior hypertrophy stimulus. Arnold Schwarzenegger famously credited donkey calf raises for his calf development. Not available at every gym — use calf press on leg press as an alternative stretch position.

exercises.name: Banded Good Morning
exercises.notes: Great entry-level hip hinge with accommodating resistance — the band is lightest at the bottom (where the spine is most vulnerable) and heaviest at the top (where you're strongest). Perfect for warm-ups, home training, or teaching the good morning pattern before loading with a barbell.

exercises.name: Banded Leg Curl (Prone)
exercises.notes: Simple, low-cost hamstring isolation — only requires a resistance band and an anchor point. The ascending resistance profile (harder at contraction) is well-suited for hamstring curls since the muscle is strongest in the shortened position. Good for warm-ups, rehab, or home training when no leg curl machine is available.

exercises.name: Snatch-Grip Deadlift
exercises.notes: The wide grip increases range of motion and forces a lower hip start position, dramatically increasing upper back, trap, and hamstring demand. A favorite of Olympic weightlifters and powerlifters for building pulling strength from the floor. Expect to use 20-30% less weight than conventional deadlift.

exercises.name: Deficit Deadlift
exercises.notes: Increases range of motion by 1-3 inches, making the pull off the floor harder. Excellent for lifters who are weak off the floor in conventional deadlifts. The extended ROM increases quad and hamstring demand. Pairs well with rack pulls — one trains the bottom, the other trains the top.

exercises.name: Pause Deadlift
exercises.notes: Eliminates the stretch-shortening cycle, forcing you to maintain tension through the sticking point. Builds positional strength and reinforces proper back position under load. If your back rounds during regular deadlifts, pause deadlifts will teach you to stay tight. Use 60-75% of 1RM.

exercises.name: Landmine Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: The landmine's arc path provides a more natural movement trajectory than a barbell RDL. The load vector changes through the range of motion (more horizontal at the bottom, more vertical at the top), which some lifters find more comfortable on the lower back. Also works well for single-leg variations.

exercises.name: Standing Dumbbell Calf Raise
exercises.notes: The most accessible loaded calf raise — no machine required, just dumbbells and a step or plate. Allows progressive overload for anyone training at home or when the calf raise machine is occupied. Can also be done single-leg for unilateral work. Full ROM is critical — get the deep stretch at the bottom and full contraction at the top.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press (Flat)
exercises.notes: Most popular upper body exercise. Wider grip = more chest stretch, narrower = more tricep emphasis. Grip around 1.5x shoulder width is a good starting point for most people. The arch is not cheating — a slight natural arch protects the shoulders.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press (Flat)
exercises.notes: Better chest stretch at bottom than barbell. Fixes left/right imbalances since each arm works independently. Many people find these produce better chest development than barbell because of the greater ROM.

exercises.name: Incline Barbell Press
exercises.notes: ~30 degrees maximizes upper chest emphasis (Lauver et al., 2016). At 45 degrees, the front deltoids take over significantly — if your gym bench only goes to 45, use dumbbells or cables for upper chest instead. Most people have a weak upper chest relative to mid/lower.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Press
exercises.notes: Many coaches consider this the single best chest exercise for complete development. Combines upper chest emphasis with a deep stretch under load — two key drivers of hypertrophy. The stretch-mediated hypertrophy research (Pedrosa et al., 2023) supports exercises that load muscles at long lengths.

exercises.name: Decline Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Targets lower chest / sternal fibers. Reduced shoulder stress compared to flat bench. Some research suggests similar overall chest activation to flat bench with less anterior deltoid involvement. Dips are a bodyweight alternative for this angle.

exercises.name: Decline Dumbbell Press
exercises.notes: Dumbbell version allows greater ROM and is easier to set up solo than barbell decline. Good option if your gym doesn't have a dedicated decline bench with a barbell rack.

exercises.name: Chest Press Machine
exercises.notes: Safest pressing option. Great for training to absolute failure without a spotter, for drop sets, and for building confidence before moving to free weights. Research shows machines and free weights produce similar hypertrophy when effort is matched.

exercises.name: Incline Chest Press Machine
exercises.notes: Not every gym has a dedicated incline press machine, but if yours does, it's an excellent beginner-friendly way to target the upper chest. Safe for going to failure. Good option on days where you want upper chest work without the setup of free weights.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Bench Press (Flat)
exercises.notes: Good spotterless option. Fixed path lets you focus on pushing hard without worrying about balance. Position the bench so the bar touches mid-chest naturally — don't force an unnatural path.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Incline Press
exercises.notes: The safest way to do heavy incline pressing alone. Very common in gyms that don't have a dedicated incline bench station. Set the bench angle to ~30 degrees — the Smith bar path works well at this angle.

exercises.name: Floor Press (Barbell or Dumbbell)
exercises.notes: The floor limits your range of motion, which has two benefits: it's much easier on the shoulders (great for people with shoulder pain during bench press), and it overloads the lockout portion. Commonly used in powerlifting as a bench press accessory. Also eliminates leg drive, making it a stricter upper body exercise.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Flye (Flat)
exercises.notes: Provides an excellent chest stretch under load, but tension drops significantly at the top of the movement. If you want constant tension throughout, cables or the pec deck are superior. The deep stretch at the bottom is the most valuable part of this exercise.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Flye
exercises.notes: Upper chest isolation without the tricep fatigue of pressing. Great as a second chest exercise after an incline press, or as a pre-exhaust before pressing. The stretch at the bottom targets the clavicular (upper) chest fibers specifically.

exercises.name: Cable Flye (Mid-Height)
exercises.notes: Superior to dumbbell flyes because the cable keeps constant tension throughout the entire range of motion — including at the top where dumbbells lose tension. Adjust pulley height: high = lower chest emphasis, mid = mid chest, low = upper chest.

exercises.name: Low-to-High Cable Flye
exercises.notes: Excellent upper chest isolation without heavy pressing. The low-to-high arc matches the fiber direction of the clavicular (upper) chest. Great as a warmup to activate upper chest before pressing, or as a finisher.

exercises.name: High-to-Low Cable Flye
exercises.notes: Distinct from the chest_crossover in that this uses a more controlled arc specifically targeting lower chest fibers. The high-to-low angle follows the sternal pec fiber line. Pairs well with low-to-high flyes for complete upper + lower chest coverage in a single cable station session.

exercises.name: Cable Crossover (High-to-Low)
exercises.notes: The high-to-low arc targets the sternal (lower) chest fibers. Great finisher after pressing. Between this, mid-height cable flyes, and low-to-high flyes, you can target every region of the chest with cables alone.

exercises.name: Pec Deck / Machine Flye
exercises.notes: Very beginner-friendly fixed path. Constant tension like cables. Focus entirely on squeezing the chest. Excellent for drop sets — just move the pin. The pec deck + reverse pec deck superset is one of the most efficient shoulder-health combos in the gym.

exercises.name: Chest Dip
exercises.notes: Loads the chest in a deep stretch at the bottom, making it an excellent hypertrophy exercise. Use the assisted dip machine or a resistance band looped under your knees if you can't do bodyweight. Add a weight belt or hold a dumbbell between your feet to progress beyond bodyweight.

exercises.name: Push-Up
exercises.notes: Most accessible chest exercise. Progression: wall → incline → floor → decline (feet elevated) → weighted. Push-ups with handles or on fists allow deeper range of motion. EMG research shows push-ups produce comparable chest activation to bench press at similar relative intensities.

exercises.name: Incline Push-Up (Hands Elevated)
exercises.notes: Better than knee push-ups for building toward full push-ups because the movement pattern is identical — you're just reducing the load. Progressively lower the surface height over weeks: wall → counter → bench → floor. Also useful as a high-rep finisher for experienced lifters.

exercises.name: Decline Push-Up (Feet Elevated)
exercises.notes: The bodyweight equivalent of an incline press — targets upper chest and front delts more than regular push-ups. Feet on a standard bench (~18 inches) provides a good challenge. Progress by adding a weight vest or elevating feet higher.

exercises.name: Wide-Grip Push-Up
exercises.notes: EMG research shows wide push-ups significantly increase pectoralis major activation compared to standard or narrow push-ups (Cogley et al., 2005). Reduced tricep demand means the chest is the primary mover. Good option for bodyweight chest emphasis when dumbbells aren't available. Slightly more shoulder stress than standard push-ups due to the wider abduction angle.

exercises.name: Archer Push-Up
exercises.notes: An advanced unilateral bodyweight pressing movement that approaches one-arm push-up difficulty. Each side bears significantly more than half the body weight, making this a true strength exercise. Requires good shoulder stability and core control. Progress from wide push-ups before attempting these.

exercises.name: Banded Push-Up
exercises.notes: Accommodating resistance from the band creates a unique strength curve — light at the bottom (where the chest is stretched) and heavy at the top (where regular push-ups are easiest). This matches the chest's force production curve and produces a different training stimulus than bodyweight alone. Great for adding progressive overload to push-ups without a weight vest.

exercises.name: Hex Press (Squeeze Press)
exercises.notes: The constant inward squeezing force creates chest activation throughout the entire ROM — unlike dumbbell flyes where tension drops at the top, or regular presses where the chest can coast at lockout. Very joint-friendly since the neutral grip and adduction force stabilize the shoulder. Good option for people with shoulder discomfort during regular pressing.

exercises.name: Squeeze Press (Dumbbell)
exercises.notes: Similar to hex press but emphasizes forceful adduction throughout the movement. The isometric adduction component recruits the sternal chest fibers maximally. Research by Schoenfeld and Contreras suggests that exercises combining horizontal adduction with pressing create superior chest activation compared to pressing alone. Shoulder-friendly due to neutral grip and no extreme abduction angles.

exercises.name: Svend Press (Plate Squeeze)
exercises.notes: A unique isometric + isotonic chest exercise. The constant squeezing force creates chest activation through horizontal adduction while the pressing motion adds shoulder flexion. Uses very light weight (5-25 lb plates) — the effort is in the squeeze, not the load. Popular as a pre-exhaust or finisher. Named after Norwegian strongman Svend Karlsen.

exercises.name: Landmine Press
exercises.notes: The natural arc path is exceptionally easy on shoulders. Great option if regular pressing causes shoulder pain. Single-arm version adds core demand. The pressing angle is somewhere between a flat and incline press, hitting both regions.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Cable Chest Press
exercises.notes: The unilateral pressing motion with cable resistance creates significant anti-rotation core demand while training the chest. Constant tension from the cable throughout the full ROM. Excellent for fixing left/right pressing imbalances. The standing position engages more stabilizers than any machine press.

exercises.name: Band Chest Flye (Standing)
exercises.notes: A band-only alternative to cable flyes for home workouts or travel. The band provides accommodating resistance — increasing tension at peak contraction where the chest is maximally shortened. Lower setup cost than cables but equally effective for chest isolation. Progress by using thicker bands or doubling up.

exercises.name: Decline Chest Press Machine
exercises.notes: Machine version of the decline press. Lower chest emphasis with the safety and convenience of a machine. Great for beginners who want to target lower chest without the setup complexity and spotter requirement of barbell decline pressing. Excellent for drop sets and training to failure.

exercises.name: Plate-Loaded Chest Press
exercises.notes: Plate-loaded machines (Hammer Strength style) offer a more natural lever-arm path than selectorized machines, often with converging handles that create an arc similar to a dumbbell press. Many models allow independent arm movement, fixing imbalances. The lever system provides a different resistance curve than cables or selectorized stacks. Can be loaded very heavy for early-workout compound pressing.

exercises.name: Close-Grip Bench Press
exercises.notes: Shoulder-width grip is ideal — going narrower adds wrist strain without extra tricep benefit. Great compound tricep builder and bench press accessory. Helps strengthen the lockout portion of the bench press.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.notes: A unique exercise that stretches both the lats and chest under load simultaneously. Whether it biases lats or chest depends partly on intent: thinking about pulling with your lats shifts the emphasis toward back, thinking about squeezing your chest shifts it toward pecs. EMG data is mixed — treat it as a general upper body exercise rather than a targeted chest or back move. Great for shoulder mobility and thoracic extension.

exercises.name: Reverse-Grip Bench Press
exercises.notes: ~30% greater upper pec (clavicular head) activation vs standard grip (Lehman 2005). One of the strongest evidence-supported bench press variations for upper chest targeting. Requires careful unracking due to supinated grip — always use a spotter or Smith machine when learning.

exercises.name: Neutral-Grip Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Neutral (palms-in) grip significantly decreases acromioclavicular compression and glenohumeral shear forces (Noteboom et al. 2024). Excellent shoulder-friendly pressing option. Slightly more tricep emphasis than pronated grip due to arm path.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: 37-41% greater external oblique activation vs bilateral pressing (Anderson & Behm 2005). Excellent for core anti-rotation training while still building chest. Also useful for addressing bilateral strength imbalances. Keep the non-working hand on your hip or extended for balance.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent-Over Row
exercises.notes: Major back builder. Grip width matters more than over/underhand for targeting: wider grip with elbows flared hits mid-traps and rhomboids, narrower grip with elbows tucked emphasizes lats. Underhand grip increases bicep involvement and allows heavier weight for some people. Torso angle also matters — more upright = more traps, more bent over = more lats.

exercises.name: Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Named after weightlifting coach Glenn Pendlay. The dead stop eliminates the stretch reflex, making every rep harder and stricter than a standard bent-over row. The parallel torso position maximizes lat recruitment. Excellent for building explosive pulling power. Requires good hip hinge mechanics — master the bent-over row first.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Row (Single-Arm)
exercises.notes: Best row for beginners — bench supports lower back. Great for fixing left/right imbalances since each side works independently. Can go quite heavy with some controlled body english for an overload effect (sometimes called a Kroc row).

exercises.name: Chest-Supported Dumbbell Row
exercises.notes: If bent-over rows hurt your lower back, use these instead. 100% back focus with zero cheating. The bench angle affects which back muscles are emphasized: steeper angle = more mid-traps/rhomboids, flatter angle = more lats.

exercises.name: Seal Row (Chest-Supported Barbell Row)
exercises.notes: The ultimate anti-cheat row. Your entire body is supported so the ONLY way to move the weight is with your back muscles. Allows heavier loading than dumbbell chest-supported rows. Setup can be awkward if your gym doesn't have a dedicated station — stacking the bench on blocks works but takes time. Growing in popularity for good reason.

exercises.name: Seated Cable Row
exercises.notes: Constant cable tension throughout the movement. Different attachments change the emphasis: V-handle (neutral grip) = more lat emphasis with elbows tucked, wide bar = more mid-traps/rhomboids with elbows flared. The cable's constant tension profile makes this a great complement to barbell rows where the resistance curve is different.

exercises.name: T-Bar Row
exercises.notes: Neutral grip allows most people to row heavier than with a barbell. Classic bodybuilding exercise. The fixed end of the barbell creates a natural arc that many find more comfortable than straight barbell rows. If your gym doesn't have a T-bar station, wedge a barbell in a corner and use a V-handle.

exercises.name: Machine Row (Plate-Loaded or Cable)
exercises.notes: Very beginner-friendly. Chest pad supports you so you can focus entirely on pulling. Plate-loaded versions (like Hammer Strength) allow independent arm movement on some models, which helps identify and fix left/right imbalances.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Row
exercises.notes: Good entry point for learning the barbell row pattern with added safety. The fixed bar path means you can't cheat by swinging the bar outward, which teaches a clean vertical pull to the torso. Pairs naturally with Smith machine pressing for efficient supersets.

exercises.name: Landmine Row (Single-Arm)
exercises.notes: The arcing bar path creates a unique pulling angle that hits the back differently from standard rows. Great variation to cycle in when standard rows feel stale. Standing alongside the bar allows different elbow tracking compared to the Meadows row variation (which is performed perpendicular to the bar).

exercises.name: Inverted Row (Bodyweight Row)
exercises.notes: The pulling equivalent of a push-up. Great stepping stone toward barbell rows and pull-ups. Can also be done with gymnastics rings or TRX straps, which add instability and allow grip rotation. Progression: high bar (easy) → low bar → feet elevated → weighted vest.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Cable Row
exercises.notes: Fixes left/right back imbalances. The slight rotation allowed at the cable station actually increases ROM and matches how the lats function (they assist with torso rotation). Constant cable tension throughout. Pairs well with single-arm lat pulldown for a complete unilateral back session.

exercises.name: Pull-Up (Overhand Grip)
exercises.notes: The ultimate back exercise. Overhand grip emphasizes the lats and teres major with less bicep involvement compared to chin-ups. Research shows similar lat activation across grip types (Youdas et al., 2010), so the best grip is the one that lets you do the most quality reps. Add weight with a belt or dumbbell between feet once bodyweight becomes easy for 12+ reps.

exercises.name: Chin-Up (Underhand Grip)
exercises.notes: Equally effective as pull-ups for lat development — EMG research shows no significant difference in lat activation between grips. The higher bicep involvement makes these a great two-for-one exercise on pull days. Usually the first bodyweight vertical pull people master due to the mechanical advantage.

exercises.name: Neutral-Grip Pull-Up
exercises.notes: The most joint-friendly pull-up variation. The neutral grip places the shoulder in a packed, stable position and eliminates the wrist/forearm rotation stress of pronated and supinated grips. If pull-ups or chin-ups bother your shoulders or elbows, try these first. Requires a bar with parallel handles (common on most pull-up stations).

exercises.name: Assisted Pull-Up Machine
exercises.notes: The bridge between lat pulldowns and real pull-ups. Track your assistance weight and reduce it by 5-10 lbs every week or two. Once you can do the movement with less than ~25% of your bodyweight in assistance, try unassisted negatives.

exercises.name: Band-Assisted Pull-Up
exercises.notes: Differs from the machine in an important way: the band provides the most assistance at the bottom (where the band is most stretched and you're weakest) and the least at the top (where the band is slack and you're strongest). This resistance profile more closely matches the actual pull-up than a machine does. Progress by using thinner bands until you don't need one.

exercises.name: Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Machine version of the pull-up. Adjustable weight makes it accessible for any level. Different grips shift emphasis slightly: wide overhand increases the stretch on the lats, close neutral increases ROM and bicep involvement, underhand mimics a chin-up. All grips produce similar lat activation — variation keeps things fresh and hits slightly different angles.

exercises.name: Close-Grip Lat Pulldown (Neutral Grip)
exercises.notes: The neutral grip is easiest on the shoulders and wrists. The longer range of motion compared to wide-grip may actually be superior for lat growth. Good default pulldown variation for anyone with shoulder or wrist discomfort.

exercises.name: Reverse-Grip Lat Pulldown (Supinated)
exercises.notes: The cable chin-up. Great for people who can't do bodyweight chin-ups yet but want the same grip and muscle emphasis. The supinated grip puts the biceps in a strong position, so most people can pull more weight than with an overhand grip. Counts as both back and bicep work — useful on pull days when time is limited.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: The best way to identify and fix left/right lat imbalances — most people have a dominant side that takes over during bilateral pulldowns. The single-arm position allows a slight rotation that increases the range of motion. Pairs naturally with single-arm cable rows for a complete unilateral back session.

exercises.name: Straight-Arm Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Isolates the lats without any bicep involvement — the biceps can't contribute because the elbow doesn't bend. Excellent for building the mind-muscle connection with your lats, which many beginners struggle with. Great as a lat activation exercise before pull-ups or pulldowns, or as a finisher after heavy rowing.

exercises.name: Pullover Machine (Nautilus-Style)
exercises.notes: Not every gym has one, but if yours does, it provides something no other exercise can: true lat isolation with a deep stretch under load through a full range of motion, all on a guided path. The machine handles the stability so you can focus entirely on the lat contraction. Originally designed by Arthur Jones for Nautilus. Having a resurgence in well-equipped gyms.

exercises.name: Seated High Cable Row (to Face)
exercises.notes: Hits upper back and rear delts simultaneously. Great for posture since it strengthens the muscles that pull your shoulders back and down. The high cable angle targets the mid-traps and rhomboids more than a standard low cable row.

exercises.name: Low Row Machine (Plate-Loaded)
exercises.notes: The converging arm path of plate-loaded low row machines (like the Hammer Strength Iso-Lateral Low Row) provides a unique pulling angle that targets the lower lats more than standard machine rows. The chest pad eliminates spinal loading, making this very beginner-friendly. Independent arms on some models help address imbalances.

exercises.name: Chest-Supported Cable Row
exercises.notes: Combines the spinal-friendly benefits of chest-supported rows with the constant tension of cables. The cable's linear resistance profile differs from dumbbells (where gravity creates the hardest point at the bottom) — cables are hardest at peak contraction, which enhances the squeeze at the top. Requires a cable station and an adjustable bench positioned in front of it.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Row (Single-Arm)
exercises.notes: The kettlebell's offset center of mass (handle above the bell) creates a unique grip and stability challenge compared to dumbbells. This additional grip demand enhances forearm development. The thicker handle also helps build grip endurance. A good option for home gyms with limited equipment.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Gorilla Row
exercises.notes: An excellent home gym or kettlebell-focused row. The dead stop from the floor eliminates momentum (similar to Pendlay rows), and the alternating pattern provides built-in core anti-rotation training. The bent-over position demands hip hinge endurance. Named for the gorilla-like stance assumed during the movement.

exercises.name: Kettlebell High Pull
exercises.notes: An explosive pulling movement that trains power production through the posterior chain and upper back simultaneously. Bridges the gap between traditional rowing and Olympic-style pulling. The high elbow position targets the upper traps and rear delts more than standard rows. Keep the kettlebell close to the body throughout — it should travel in a straight line, not swing out in an arc.

exercises.name: Inverted Row (Bar or Rings)
exercises.notes: Differs from the Smith machine bodyweight row by using a fixed bar or rings, which allows more natural grip rotation and shoulder movement. Rings add an instability component that increases core and rotator cuff demand. An excellent progression exercise: high bar → low bar → feet elevated → rings → weighted vest. Useful for home gyms without machines.

exercises.name: Superman (Prone Back Extension Hold)
exercises.notes: A bodyweight spinal extension exercise that strengthens the erector spinae, glutes, and posterior chain without any equipment. The isometric hold variation builds endurance in the postural muscles. Can also be performed dynamically (lift and lower for reps) for more volume. A staple in physical therapy programs for lower back strengthening. Avoid if you have acute lower back pain — try bird dogs instead as a regression.

exercises.name: Resistance Band Row
exercises.notes: The most accessible rowing exercise — requires only a resistance band, which can be used anywhere. The ascending resistance curve (hardest at peak contraction) complements free-weight rows (hardest at the stretch). Excellent for warm-ups, travel workouts, rehabilitation, and home training. Use thicker bands for more resistance.

exercises.name: Band Face Pull
exercises.notes: The band version of the cable face pull — equally effective for rear delt and rotator cuff health. The ascending resistance of the band matches the strength curve of external rotation well. Can be done anywhere with a band and an anchor point, making it perfect for warm-ups and travel. Should be in every program regardless of training level for shoulder health and posture (Schoenfeld, 2010).

exercises.name: Meadows Row (Single-Arm Landmine Row)
exercises.notes: Named after the late bodybuilder John Meadows (Mountain Dog Training). The perpendicular stance and overhand grip on the fat end of the barbell create a unique pulling angle that emphasizes the upper lats and teres major with a deep stretch at the bottom of each rep. The thick bar grip challenges forearm strength. More advanced than a standard landmine row due to the stance and grip demands. A favorite in bodybuilding programs for targeting the upper/outer lats.

exercises.name: Yates Row (Underhand Barbell Row)
exercises.notes: Named after 6x Mr. Olympia Dorian Yates, who made this his primary back builder. The supinated grip increases bicep involvement and allows heavier loading for some lifters. The more upright torso angle (compared to Pendlay rows) reduces lower back stress while shifting emphasis to the lower lats. Note: the supinated grip puts more stress on the bicep tendons — warm up properly and avoid jerking the weight.

exercises.name: Cable Shrug
exercises.notes: The cable's constant tension makes this superior to dumbbell or barbell shrugs for some lifters, as the resistance doesn't disappear at the top of the movement. The angle of the cable can be adjusted — low cables pull down and slightly forward, which can increase range of motion compared to vertical-only shrugs. Lighter weight with a slow tempo and peak contraction hold tends to work better than heavy momentum shrugs for trap hypertrophy.

exercises.name: Snatch-Grip Barbell Row
exercises.notes: The ultra-wide grip shifts the row emphasis from the lats to the mid-traps, rhomboids, and rear delts — essentially a row and a face pull in one movement. Also builds significant grip and forearm strength due to the wide hand spacing. Popular in Olympic weightlifting accessory work to strengthen the upper back position for snatches. The wider grip reduces the weight you can row by 20-30% compared to a standard-width row, so ego-check accordingly.

exercises.name: Cable Pullover (Standing)
exercises.notes: Similar to the straight-arm pulldown but performed with a wider arc and slight hip hinge that allows a deeper overhead stretch. The cable provides constant tension through the entire range, unlike dumbbell pullovers where gravity reduces tension at the bottom. Targets the lats through shoulder extension without bicep involvement. An excellent lat isolation exercise for those who don't have a pullover machine.

exercises.name: V-Bar Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Similar lat activation to front pulldown with neutral grip being more joint-friendly (Sperandei 2009). Lower posterior deltoid activation than wide-grip variants. Excellent option for those with shoulder mobility limitations or wrist discomfort.

exercises.name: Rope Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Rope attachment allows natural wrist rotation throughout the ROM and enables a wider split at the bottom for greater lat contraction. Lower loading potential than bar attachments but excellent for mind-muscle connection and as a finisher. Grip tends to be the limiting factor.

exercises.name: Wide-Grip Cable Row
exercises.notes: Wide grip shifts emphasis from lats to mid-traps and rear deltoids. Elbows-out position mimics a face pull pattern but with heavier loading potential. Useful for those who want more upper back thickness without the spinal load of bent-over rows.

exercises.name: V-Bar Cable Row
exercises.notes: Neutral close grip emphasizes lats over mid-traps. The V-bar is the most common cable row attachment and is very wrist-friendly. Most people can handle slightly more weight with the V-bar than with a straight bar due to the mechanically advantageous grip position.

exercises.name: Close-Grip Cable Row (Supinated)
exercises.notes: Close supinated grip maximizes lower lat and bicep involvement. The underhand grip is similar to a Yates row but in a seated position, removing spinal load. Excellent for those who want lat emphasis without taxing the lower back. Biceps will fatigue before lats for most people.

exercises.name: Weighted Pull-Up
exercises.notes: The progression from bodyweight pull-ups once 12+ reps becomes easy. One of the best exercises for building back width and lat strength. Adding weight forces recruitment of more motor units in the lats and teres major. Can be loaded with a dip belt, weight vest, chains, or a dumbbell between the feet. Squeeze glutes and brace abs to prevent lower spine hyperextension under load.

exercises.name: Weighted Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Combines heavy lat work with significant bicep loading — an efficient two-for-one exercise. Research (Youdas et al., 2010) shows similar lat activation to pull-ups, but chin-ups produce higher bicep EMG activity. Most people can load heavier than weighted pull-ups due to the bicep mechanical advantage. Excellent for both back strength and arm hypertrophy.

exercises.name: Barbell Overhead Press (Standing)
exercises.notes: Best overall shoulder builder. Standing requires significantly more core than seated — the core demand is one of the main advantages. If you find yourself leaning back excessively, the weight is too heavy. The strict standing press is one of the best measures of real-world upper body strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press (Seated)
exercises.notes: Easier to learn than barbell. Dumbbells allow a more natural pressing arc and greater range of motion. The back support removes the core demand — this isn't worse, it just lets you focus entirely on the shoulders and use more weight. Good default pressing option for most trainees.

exercises.name: Arnold Press
exercises.notes: Named after Arnold Schwarzenegger. The rotation through the pressing motion means the front delt works at the bottom (palms toward you) and the side delt gets more work through the middle and top of the press. Effectively combines a front raise into a shoulder press. Uses lighter weight than standard presses due to the mechanical disadvantage of the rotation.

exercises.name: Shoulder Press Machine
exercises.notes: Safest overhead press. Great for beginners learning the pressing pattern, for training to failure safely, and for drop sets. Some machines offer converging paths that feel more natural than straight-up tracks.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Safe overhead pressing alone without a spotter. The fixed path eliminates the stabilization demand, letting you focus on pure overhead pressing force. Allows heavier loading than free-weight presses for some people. Good option for gyms without a dedicated shoulder press machine.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Overhead Press (Single-Arm)
exercises.notes: The kettlebell's offset center of mass forces greater rotator cuff and core stabilization compared to a dumbbell press. Single-arm work also addresses left-right strength imbalances. A staple in kettlebell training programs (StrongFirst, RKC). The rack position itself builds shoulder and thoracic mobility over time.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Push Press
exercises.notes: The push press uses leg drive to move heavier loads overhead than a strict press. This overloads the eccentric (lowering) phase — you press with legs but lower under strict control. Popular in CrossFit and strength sports. The leg dip component also builds explosive power transfer from lower to upper body. Use when a strict press stalls.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Bottoms-Up Press
exercises.notes: The ultimate shoulder stabilizer exercise. The inverted position creates massive rotator cuff and grip demand — the shoulder stabilizers fire at near-maximal levels to keep the bell balanced. Used extensively in physical therapy and return-to-sport protocols. Also excellent for improving shoulder proprioception. Load is necessarily light (typically 50-60% of strict press weight), so joint stress is low despite the difficulty rating. Gray Cook and FMS popularized this as a corrective exercise.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Halo
exercises.notes: Excellent warmup and mobility exercise that takes the shoulder through its full range of motion under light load. Builds shoulder stability in all planes — flexion, extension, abduction, and rotation are all trained in one movement. Often used in kettlebell programs as the first exercise to prepare the shoulders for pressing. Also trains thoracic spine mobility as you rotate to pass the bell behind your head. Use a light KB (8-12 kg for most people).

exercises.name: Pike Push-Up
exercises.notes: The best bodyweight substitute for overhead pressing. The inverted V position shifts most of your body weight onto the shoulders and triceps, mimicking an overhead press. A key stepping stone toward handstand push-ups. Elevating the feet on a bench (decline pike push-up) further increases the vertical pressing angle and difficulty. For home gym setups without heavy enough dumbbells, this is an excellent shoulder builder.

exercises.name: Wall Handstand Push-Up
exercises.notes: The most demanding bodyweight vertical push exercise. Pressing your entire body weight overhead requires serious shoulder, tricep, and core strength. EMG studies show similar deltoid activation to heavy barbell overhead pressing. The wall provides balance support — freestanding handstand push-ups are a gymnastic skill beyond what most gym-goers need. A reasonable progression: pike push-up → feet-elevated pike push-up → wall handstand push-up. This exercise alone can build impressive shoulders without any equipment.

exercises.name: Resistance Band Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Great option for home training, travel, rehab, and warmups. The ascending resistance profile (harder at the top) is actually beneficial for lockout strength. Bands also eliminate the deceleration phase that free weights have — you must push hard through the entire range. Combine with band lateral raises and band pull-aparts for a complete shoulder workout with just one $10 band.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: THE exercise for wider shoulders. The side delts are the most visible part of the shoulder and respond well to high volume (15-20+ sets per week across the training week). Recent research on lengthened partials suggests that the bottom portion of the movement (where the side delt is stretched) may be the most growth-producing part — so don't neglect the bottom half of the range. #1 mistake = going too heavy and turning it into a trap exercise.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Better constant tension than dumbbells, especially at the bottom of the movement where the side delt is in a stretched position. The behind-the-body cable path provides tension even at the starting position. One arm at a time allows better mind-muscle connection. Many coaches consider this superior to dumbbell laterals for side delt growth.

exercises.name: Machine Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Not every gym has one. If yours does, it's excellent for strict side delt isolation — impossible to cheat. Great for drop sets since you just move the pin. The fixed path means you can train closer to failure with less injury risk.

exercises.name: Resistance Band Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Accessible side delt training requiring only a resistance band. The ascending resistance profile is actually a good match for lateral raises — the side delt gets progressively stronger through abduction up to about 90 degrees. Ideal for home workouts, hotel training, and warmup sets before heavier dumbbell or cable laterals. High-rep band laterals (20-25 reps) are an excellent metabolic stress finisher.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Front Raise
exercises.notes: Often unnecessary if you already bench press and overhead press — both hit the front delts heavily. The front delt is typically the most developed part of the shoulder in most trainees due to all the pressing they do. Add front raises only if your front delts are specifically underdeveloped (rare) or for physique balance.

exercises.name: Cable Front Raise
exercises.notes: Cable version maintains tension at the bottom of the movement where dumbbells have none. Same caveat as dumbbell front raises applies — most people don't need direct front delt work. If you do add them, the cable version is mechanically superior to dumbbells.

exercises.name: Plate Front Raise
exercises.notes: A simple front raise variation using a weight plate instead of dumbbells. The wide grip position slightly increases side delt and upper chest involvement compared to a neutral-grip dumbbell front raise. Some lifters raise the plate overhead for extra range of motion. Good as a finisher or burnout set. The plate's grip demands also provide mild forearm work.

exercises.name: Lu Raise (Front-to-Lateral Raise)
exercises.notes: Named after Chinese Olympic weightlifter Lu Xiaojun, who popularized this exercise in training footage. Combines a front raise with a lateral raise in one continuous movement, hitting both front and side delts with extended time under tension. The transition from front to lateral position also works the often-neglected area between the front and side delt heads. Use very light dumbbells (5-15 lbs) — the continuous tension accumulates rapidly. An excellent shoulder finisher.

exercises.name: Face Pull (Cable)
exercises.notes: The single most important exercise for shoulder health in any program. Trains external rotation and scapular retraction — the exact movements that counterbalance all the internal rotation from pressing. Should be in EVERY program, regardless of goals. Do 2-4 sets of 15-20 on every upper body or push day. The rear delts and external rotators can handle very high weekly volume without recovery issues.

exercises.name: Reverse Fly Machine (Rear Pec Deck)
exercises.notes: Same machine as pec deck but you sit the other way. One of the most efficient supersets in the gym: chest flyes → immediately turn around → reverse flyes for rear delts. Great for drop sets since you just move the pin. The fixed path ensures strict rear delt isolation.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Flye (Bent-Over)
exercises.notes: Targets rear delts — the most commonly underdeveloped part of the shoulder. Light weights and high reps are the key. The rear delts are small muscles that fatigue quickly but also recover quickly, so high frequency (3-4x per week) works well. Can also be done lying face down on an incline bench to eliminate any momentum.

exercises.name: Cable Rear Delt Flye
exercises.notes: Constant cable tension throughout the entire movement — unlike dumbbells which have no tension at the bottom and peak tension only at the top. The cables also allow easy angle adjustment to find the exact line of pull that hits your rear delts best. Pairs naturally with cable lateral raises for an efficient cable-only shoulder finisher.

exercises.name: Prone Incline Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: The prone position on an incline bench completely eliminates body English and momentum, making this one of the strictest lateral raise variations. The incline angle also shifts the resistance curve — the side delt is loaded more heavily in the stretched (bottom) position compared to standing laterals, which aligns with recent lengthened-partial hypertrophy research (Pedrosa et al., 2023). Also hits the rear delts more than standing laterals due to the prone position. A favorite of evidence-based coaches like Jeff Nippard and Mike Israetel.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Apart
exercises.notes: Do these every single training day as a warmup or between pressing sets. 2-3 sets of 15-30 reps costs almost nothing in terms of fatigue but accumulates massive rear delt and external rotator volume over time. A resistance band is a $10 investment that can save your shoulders. The simplest and most effective prehab tool in the gym.

exercises.name: Prone Y-Raise
exercises.notes: Targets the lower traps and the rotator cuff — two muscle groups that are critical for shoulder health but rarely trained directly. The lower traps depress and upwardly rotate the scapula, which is essential for safe overhead pressing. Widely used in physical therapy and sports medicine. If you have any history of shoulder impingement or pain, these should be a staple. Can also do T-raises (arms straight to sides) and W-raises (bent elbows, external rotation) in the same position for complete scapular stabilizer coverage.

exercises.name: Cable Y-Raise
exercises.notes: The cable version of the Y-raise provides constant tension throughout the movement, unlike the prone dumbbell version where tension drops at the bottom. Targets the lower trapezius and rotator cuff — critical stabilizers for overhead pressing and shoulder health. The cable allows precise angle adjustment. Often used in physical therapy settings for scapular dyskinesis and impingement rehabilitation. Combine with cable external rotations for a complete rotator cuff circuit.

exercises.name: Cable External Rotation
exercises.notes: The most targeted rotator cuff exercise available. Specifically trains the infraspinatus and teres minor — the primary external rotators that counterbalance the much stronger internal rotators (pecs, lats, subscapularis). Essential prehab for anyone who does heavy bench pressing or overhead work. Also a key rehab exercise for rotator cuff tendinopathy. The cable provides constant tension throughout the rotation arc. Recommended by ACSM and NSCA as a foundational shoulder health exercise. Use light loads — the external rotators are small muscles.

exercises.name: Cable Internal Rotation
exercises.notes: Trains the subscapularis — the primary internal rotator of the shoulder and the largest rotator cuff muscle. While internal rotation is heavily trained by pressing movements, direct internal rotation work can address specific weaknesses in throwing athletes, swimmers, and anyone recovering from shoulder instability. Most lifters should prioritize external rotation work over internal, but pairing both in a rotator cuff circuit ensures balanced rotator cuff strength. Common in sports medicine protocols.

exercises.name: Upright Row (Cable or Dumbbell)
exercises.notes: Controversial exercise. The concern is subacromial impingement — when you pull a weight upward with internally rotated shoulders (narrow grip, high pull), the supraspinatus tendon can get pinched. The FIX: use a wide grip (reduces internal rotation), pull to chest height only (not chin), and use cables or dumbbells instead of a barbell (allows natural wrist rotation). With these modifications, the exercise is reasonably safe and hits both side delts and upper traps. If it still irritates your shoulders, replace with lateral raises + shrugs — you get the same muscles without the risk.

exercises.name: Barbell Shrug
exercises.notes: The upper traps respond well to heavy loads with moderate reps. However, many people find that heavy deadlifts and rows already build their traps sufficiently without direct shrug work. Add shrugs if you want extra trap development or if you don't deadlift. Focus on the squeeze at the top, not the weight.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shrug
exercises.notes: Dumbbells at your sides allow a slightly greater range of motion than a barbell in front. The neutral hand position is also more comfortable for most people. Pair with farmer's walks for complete trap development — shrugs hit the upper traps, heavy carries build the mid and lower traps.

exercises.name: Trap Bar Shrug
exercises.notes: Allows the heaviest shrug loading of any variation — the centered weight and neutral grip eliminate the barbell's tendency to pull you forward or the dumbbell's tendency to bump your thighs. The trap bar was literally named after the trapezius muscle. If your gym has one, this is the optimal shrug tool.

exercises.name: Behind-Body Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Loads the medial deltoid in the lengthened (stretched) position where dumbbells provide zero resistance. The behind-body cable angle shifts the resistance curve to maximize tension at the bottom of the ROM. One of the best lateral raise variations for complete medial delt development.

exercises.name: Seated Dumbbell Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Eliminates momentum and leg drive, forcing the medial deltoid to do all the work. Typically requires 20-30% less weight than standing lateral raises. Excellent for those who tend to swing the weight or struggle with strict form on standing raises.

exercises.name: Incline Lying Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: The incline angle creates constant tension through the entire range of motion, similar to the behind-body cable lateral raise but with dumbbells. Eliminates all momentum. Particularly effective for the stretched position of the medial deltoid. Use 40-50% of your normal lateral raise weight.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Upright Row
exercises.notes: Dumbbells allow each arm to find its natural path, reducing the shoulder impingement risk inherent in barbell upright rows. The freer movement path means the supraspinatus is less likely to get pinched in the subacromial space. Stop at or below parallel to further reduce risk.

exercises.name: Wide-Grip Cable Upright Row
exercises.notes: Wide grip maximizes deltoid and trap activation while significantly reducing shoulder impingement risk vs narrow grip (McAllister et al. 2013). The cable provides constant tension through the ROM. Excellent middle ground between the impingement risk of barbell upright rows and the lower loading of lateral raises.

exercises.name: Barbell Curl
exercises.notes: The standard bilateral bicep exercise. Allows the heaviest curl loading. If wrists hurt with a straight bar, switch to the EZ bar — the angled grip reduces wrist extension stress. Elbows pinned at your sides hits the biceps through their mid-range; letting elbows drift forward shifts to a shortened-position emphasis.

exercises.name: EZ Bar Curl
exercises.notes: Preferred by most people over straight barbell for wrist comfort. The slight pronation of the wrist shifts a small amount of work to the brachialis and brachioradialis, which some argue makes it a slightly more complete arm exercise than the straight bar. Practically, the difference is minimal — use whichever bar feels better on your joints.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bicep Curl
exercises.notes: The most versatile bicep exercise. The wrist supination (rotating palm upward during the curl) maximizes bicep activation because the bicep is a supinator as well as an elbow flexor. Dumbbells also reveal and fix left/right imbalances. Can be done seated to eliminate all momentum.

exercises.name: Hammer Curl
exercises.notes: Essential for arm thickness and a well-developed look from all angles. The biceps have two main functions: elbow flexion AND forearm supination. With a neutral grip you take supination out of the equation, so the brachialis (which can only flex the elbow) does proportionally more work. Do BOTH hammer curls AND supinated curls for complete arm development. Most people are stronger on hammer curls than regular curls.

exercises.name: Preacher Curl
exercises.notes: The angled pad with shoulders flexed forward places the bicep in a slightly shortened position, so the hardest part of the movement is in the mid-range and contracted position. This makes it a good complement to incline curls (which emphasize the stretched position). Don't fully extend at the bottom under heavy load — stop just short of lockout to protect the distal bicep tendon.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
exercises.notes: Growing body of research on stretch-mediated hypertrophy suggests that exercises loading a muscle in its lengthened position may produce superior growth. The incline curl is one of the best examples of this for the biceps — the shoulder extension at the bottom creates a deep stretch on the long head under load. Use a full range of motion and don't cut the bottom short. The long head is the outer part of the bicep that creates the peaked look.

exercises.name: Bayesian Cable Curl (Behind-Body Cable Curl)
exercises.notes: The cable version of the incline curl concept — loads the bicep long head in a stretched position with constant cable tension throughout. Unlike incline dumbbell curls (where tension drops at the top), the cable maintains resistance across the entire range of motion, including the peak contraction. Increasingly popular due to stretch-mediated hypertrophy research. Arguably the single best bicep isolation exercise for long head growth when combining stretch loading with constant tension.

exercises.name: Spider Curl
exercises.notes: The counterpart to incline curls. Where incline curls load the stretched position, spider curls load the shortened/contracted position. The vertical arm position means peak tension occurs when the bicep is fully contracted — where most other curls have the least tension. For complete bicep development across the entire range of motion, include exercises from both ends: incline or bayesian curls (stretch-emphasis) plus spider or concentration curls (contraction-emphasis).

exercises.name: Cable Curl
exercises.notes: Constant tension profile makes this an excellent finisher after heavier free-weight curls. Different attachments shift emphasis slightly: straight bar for heavy bilateral work, rope for neutral grip and peak contraction squeeze, D-handles for unilateral work. The cable's direction of pull (angled toward the pulley) provides a unique resistance curve compared to free weights.

exercises.name: Rope Cable Curl (Hammer Grip)
exercises.notes: Combines the benefits of hammer curls (brachialis and brachioradialis emphasis) with constant cable tension. The rope also allows you to spread the ends at peak contraction, which some people find gives a better squeeze than a fixed handle. Good finishing exercise after heavier free-weight curls.

exercises.name: Concentration Curl
exercises.notes: One of the highest-activation bicep exercises due to the completely isolated, cheat-free position. An ACE-sponsored EMG study ranked it #1 for bicep activation, though EMG studies have limitations — activation doesn't always perfectly predict growth. The main advantage is the mind-muscle connection: the braced position and visual contact with the working muscle make it very easy to feel the bicep working. Great for beginners learning to "feel" their biceps.

exercises.name: Reverse Curl (EZ Bar or Barbell)
exercises.notes: The most direct exercise for the brachioradialis — the thick forearm muscle on the thumb side that's visible when your arms are at your sides. This muscle is responsible for the 'thick forearm' look and is only partially hit by hammer curls (neutral grip) and barely hit by regular curls (supinated grip). Reverse curls also strengthen the wrist extensors isometrically, which helps prevent tennis elbow. Include if forearm size is a priority.

exercises.name: Cable Tricep Pushdown (Rope or Bar)
exercises.notes: The most popular tricep exercise. Primarily targets the lateral and medial heads of the tricep because the shoulder is in a neutral position (not overhead). The rope attachment allows wrist rotation at the bottom for a harder peak contraction. Straight bar allows heavier loading. For complete tricep development, PAIR pushdowns with an overhead movement (overhead extensions or skull crushers) to also hit the long head in its stretched position.

exercises.name: Overhead Tricep Extension (Dumbbell, Two-Hand)
exercises.notes: The overhead position stretches the long head of the tricep — the biggest of the three tricep heads, running from the elbow up to the shoulder blade. Pushdowns alone don't fully load the long head because the shoulder stays neutral. Research consistently shows that training a muscle in its lengthened position produces more growth (Maeo et al., 2021), making overhead extensions essential for complete tricep development. Pair with pushdowns: pushdowns for lateral/medial heads, overhead for the long head.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Overhead Extension
exercises.notes: Unilateral version for identifying and correcting left/right tricep imbalances. The single-arm position allows slightly more range of motion and a deeper stretch than the two-hand version. Also useful when you don't have a dumbbell heavy enough for the two-hand version — one arm at a time effectively doubles the relative load.

exercises.name: Skull Crusher (Lying Tricep Extension)
exercises.notes: Classic intermediate tricep builder. The behind-head variation is sometimes called a 'French press' and is preferred because it increases the stretch on the long head. Use an EZ bar for wrist comfort. The lying position allows you to use heavier weight than standing overhead extensions since you don't need to stabilize your torso. Some people experience elbow pain — if so, switch to overhead cable extensions which have a smoother resistance curve.

exercises.name: Tricep Dip (Upright Body)
exercises.notes: The heaviest bodyweight tricep exercise. Use the assisted dip machine or a resistance band if you can't do full bodyweight. Progress by adding a weight belt or holding a dumbbell between your feet. The upright torso position is the key tricep emphasis cue — as soon as you lean forward, the chest takes over.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Tricep Kickback
exercises.notes: EMG shows high tricep activation when performed correctly — the key is fully extending the elbow at the top and holding. The problem is that most people use too much weight and never reach full lockout, which defeats the purpose. The shoulder-extended position (arm behind body) shortens the long head, so this exercise primarily targets the lateral head. Think of it as a complement to overhead extensions, not a replacement.

exercises.name: Cable Overhead Tricep Extension
exercises.notes: Arguably the best long head tricep exercise. The cable maintains tension even in the fully stretched position (behind your head), where dumbbells and barbells lose tension due to the gravity angle. The constant tension through the stretch is exactly what the stretch-mediated hypertrophy research suggests is most effective for muscle growth. If you only pick one overhead tricep movement, make it this one.

exercises.name: Diamond Push-Up (Close-Hand Push-Up)
exercises.notes: EMG research consistently shows this as one of the highest tricep activation exercises. No equipment needed — excellent for home training or as a finisher at the end of a gym session. The close hand position shifts work from the chest to the triceps compared to a standard push-up. Progression: knees → incline → floor → feet elevated → weighted vest.

exercises.name: Wrist Curl (Barbell or Dumbbell)
exercises.notes: Targets the wrist flexors on the palm side of the forearm. ALWAYS do grip and forearm work LAST in your workout — fatigued forearms will limit pulling exercises like rows, deadlifts, and curls. Most people will develop adequate forearm strength from heavy pulling exercises alone. Add direct wrist work if forearm size is a specific goal or if grip is limiting your pulling.

exercises.name: Reverse Wrist Curl
exercises.notes: Important for balanced forearm development and preventing overuse injuries like tennis elbow (lateral epicondylitis). The wrist extensors are often neglected because they're not visible like the flexors, but they're critical for joint health. If you do any wrist curls, always pair them with reverse wrist curls to maintain balance across the joint.

exercises.name: Farmer's Walk (Loaded Carry)
exercises.notes: One of the best exercises most people never do. Builds grip, traps, core stability, and mental toughness simultaneously. Also trains your body to maintain posture under load while moving — which is how your body actually functions in real life. Variations: single-arm (suitcase carry) adds anti-lateral flexion core work, overhead carry adds shoulder stability. The trap bar is the best implement for heavy farmer's walks.

exercises.name: Dead Hang
